steps.text: Long ago, a small dragon named Pim lived high above {room}. {toy} settles in to listen.
Pim could not breathe fire — only puffs of warm steam. The other dragons giggled.
One night Pim saw a tiny lantern bobbing across the valley. Someone was lost.
Pim's wings trembled. The valley was {adjective} and full of shadows.
What does Pim do?
Pim flew down and puffed warm steam — a soft cloud the lost traveler could follow home.
The older dragons came roaring, but Pim's tiny voice was the one the traveler heard first. The end.
{toy} stands at the edge of a meadow. The old river song calls from far away.
The orchard smells like apples. A friendly fox tips its hat and points the way onward.
The stones are cool and smooth. {toy} hops from one to the next, counting to {count}.
Both paths meet at a wooden bridge. The river hums underneath, sleepy and slow.
{toy} sits on the bridge and listens. The river's song says: thank you for coming. The end.
{toy} found a {adjective} door at the back of {room}. It needed a key.
An old turtle whispered: the key is hidden where laughter is loudest.
{toy} listened. From the kitchen came a giggle. From the garden came a shout.
Both places were full of friends. Both were full of noise.
Which way does {toy} go?
In the kitchen, a baby mouse held the key in its tiny paws. It traded the key for a crumb of bread.
In the garden, the key dangled from a sunflower. {toy} thanked the flower with a bow. The end.
Someone has been taking one slice of cake every night. {toy} decides to investigate.
From behind the {prop}, {toy} sees a small badger tiptoe in, bow politely, and pick up a slice.
Near the window, {toy} sees a small badger arrive, pause to wipe its paws on the mat, and step inside.
The note says 'please share' — and in the morning, {toy} finds a tiny thank-you drawn in crumbs.
The badger isn't a thief at all — it's a baker bringing samples to taste. They share a slice together. The end.
Far above the clouds swam a whale named Drift. On her back, she carried a basket of stars.
Each night Drift placed one star in the sky. Tonight her basket was almost empty.
Only one star was left — and it was the smallest, shyest one of all.
The little star whispered: 'I'm too small. No one will see me.'
Drift smiled. 'The smallest stars,' she said, 'are the ones children wish on.'
Where does Drift place the little star?
The star settled above {room} and twinkled softly. {toy} saw it through the window and made a wish. The end.
Deep in the forest, all the sleepy animals looked up and smiled at the new little light. The end.
{toy} couldn't sleep. The night was too quiet.
A moth landed on the windowsill. 'I know a song for quiet nights,' it whispered.
Does {toy} learn the song?
{toy} hummed and the moth hummed back. The room filled with a soft, sleepy buzz. The end.
{toy} listened with eyes closing slowly. The moth sang until {toy} dreamed of warm sunlight. The end.
It's market day! {toy} has three coins and a list of one item: a {adjective} something for a friend.
The cheese mouse offers a wedge that smells like sunshine. But it costs all three coins.
The ribbon shop has every color. {toy} picks a {adjective} ribbon for two coins.
The puzzle stall has a tiny wooden box that clicks when you {action_verb} it. One coin only.
{toy} sits by the fountain and wraps the gift carefully. A friend will be very happy. The end.
{toy} discovered an attic door above {room} that no one had opened in years.
Dust hung in the air like floating sugar. The ladder creaked.
Inside the attic, {toy} found three boxes labeled 'memories,' 'maybe-someday,' and 'do not open.'
Which box does {toy} open?
Inside were photos of a younger {toy} and a tiny note: 'Remember to be brave.' {toy} smiled and tucked the note in a pocket. The end.
Inside was a folded paper kite, never flown. 'Today,' {toy} decided, 'is someday.' The end.
A small white fox lived where the snow never melted. Her favorite thing was counting flakes.
Tonight, the sky dropped flakes faster than her paws could count. She got to {count} and lost track.
{toy} arrived bundled in a scarf and offered to help count.
Together they counted slowly, two by two, until their voices got drowsy.
What happens when they reach the last flake?

choices.label: They make a tiny snow nest

steps.text: They built a soft nest of snow and curled up under the stars. The fox dreamed of summer. The end.
Steam from the cocoa fogged their noses. The fox fell asleep mid-sip, paw still wrapped around the cup. The end.
{toy} found a small robot crouched between the tomato plants. It was not moving.
On its chest blinked a tiny red light. Beside it lay a {prop} it had been holding.
{toy} read the side of the robot. It said: 'GENTLE HANDS ONLY.'
How does {toy} help?
The dirt came off in soft clouds. The robot's eyes lit up green and it bowed gently. 'Friend,' it said. The end.
The light turned blue and a tiny voice sang 'thank you.' The robot stood up and watered the tomatoes. The end.
Old Marlo was the keeper of a tiny seaside lighthouse. {toy} came to visit one foggy night.
Marlo's lantern was warm and bright, but the room was full of {adjective} clutter.
On a high shelf sat a wooden box Marlo never opened. {toy} asked about it gently.
Marlo sighed. 'Inside is a song my mother taught me. I'm afraid I've forgotten it.'
Together they reached for the box. The lid creaked open.
What's inside?
A tiny crank turned and the song played itself. Marlo's eyes filled. 'There you are,' Marlo whispered. The end.
The letter held the lyrics. Marlo read them aloud and remembered the tune in the middle of the third line. The end.
{toy} stood before three bridges leading to a castle on a hill. Each bridge whispered a different promise.
A friendly troll asked: what has hands but cannot wave? {toy} guessed: a clock! The troll laughed and stepped aside.
Bells rang as {toy} stepped on each plank. By the end, {toy} was humming along with the bridge.
Each mirror showed {toy} a little braver than the last. The final mirror smiled and said, 'Go on, then.'
All three bridges led to the same gate. The castle was warm inside, and full of friends waiting. The end.
{toy} found a small smooth pebble that hummed when held tightly.
The pebble's hum changed depending on what {toy} thought about.
What does {toy} think about?
The pebble sang of pines and rain. {toy} closed its eyes and walked through tall green halls in its mind. The end.
The pebble hummed soft and slow, like a remembered laugh. {toy} smiled and tucked the pebble away. The end.
Past midnight, the library belonged to an old owl named Quill. {toy} had a special pass.
Quill led {toy} between the tall shelves. The books rustled softly, awake and listening.
'Tonight,' said Quill, 'you may pick any one book to read. But choose with your heart.'
Which book does {toy} pick?
Inside were maps to places that didn't exist yet — places {toy} would invent in dreams. The end.
It was a story of a tired bear who wanted to nap. By page three, {toy} was yawning along. The end.
{toy} flew a paper kite on a blustery hill. The string slipped — and the kite was gone.
Where does {toy} look first?
A squirrel had caught the kite and was using it as a blanket. The squirrel agreed to share — half the day each.
The kite floated like a tiny boat. A frog rode on top, happy as could be. {toy} waved hello.
The kite was tangled in tall grass. A rabbit was nibbling the string. {toy} thanked the rabbit politely.
{toy} carried the kite home, not in a hurry. The wind blew gentle now, like it was tired too. The end.
A small mouse named Ash lived in the chimney of {room}. He was very proud of his soot-grey coat.
One evening, Ash noticed something strange — his {body_part} was glowing.
Not a lot. Just a little. Like a candle had been lit inside him.
What does Ash do?
In the dark chimney, the glow lit up the whole flue. Ash was a tiny lighthouse. He felt brave. The end.
{toy} gasped softly. 'You're magic,' {toy} whispered. Ash felt nine feet tall, even though he was three inches. The end.

templates.title: The lost shoe and the mountain

steps.text: A traveler reached the foot of a tall mountain wearing only one shoe.
The other shoe had been carried off by a magpie three valleys ago.
{toy} met the traveler at a crossroads and offered to help.
How do they get up the mountain?
High on a ledge sat the nest, lined with shiny things — including the shoe. The magpie traded it for a button. The end.
{toy} and the traveler hopped one-shoe-style all the way up, laughing the whole climb. At the top: a beautiful view. The end.
Grandmother said tonight's soup would sing — but only if the right things went in. {toy} chose first.

choices.label: Mushroom and a sprig of thyme

steps.text: The soup hummed orange and sweet, like an autumn afternoon. Grandmother nodded approval.
The soup whistled in three short notes — a tune Grandmother had not heard since she was small.
The soup sang in a low, woody voice, like a tree at night. {toy} listened with eyes closed.
All the soups taste like home, Grandmother said. {toy} understood: the song was made of love. The end.
{toy} folded a tiny paper boat and set it in the gutter after a rainstorm.
The boat sailed away faster than expected. {toy} jogged alongside, watching.
At the corner, the boat met a leaf — and a tiny crew climbed aboard.
Three ladybugs and one beetle. They saluted {toy} solemnly.
What happens at the storm drain?
The crew disembarked, bowed, and marched off into the grass on important ladybug business. The end.
The boat slipped into the dark and away. {toy} imagined oceans for it. Maybe rivers. Maybe further. The end.

templates.title: The baker who woke the sun

steps.text: Long before sunrise, a baker named Pip kneaded dough in a warm dark kitchen.
Pip had a secret: every morning, the smell of baking bread woke the sun itself.
But this morning, Pip overslept. The sun had not risen.
{toy} ran to the bakery to help.
What do they bake?
The smell rose up like a hug. The sun yawned, stretched, and climbed into the sky pink and pleased. The end.
Crusty and golden — the sun woke gently and smiled at the world. {toy} got the first warm slice. The end.
{toy} found a hidden door behind the {prop} in {room}. It only opened when you whispered.
The door swung open onto a sunlit garden where every flower glowed. {toy} stepped inside, careful not to wake the bees.
The door creaked and showed a long hallway lined with books. Each one knew the answer to something.
The door opened on a quiet room where someone {toy} had not seen in a long time was waiting with cocoa.
Whatever lay inside, it was meant for {toy}. The door always finds the right somewhere. The end.
Low tide opened a secret sea cave below the cliffs. {toy} climbed down with a tide chart and a sense of wonder.
The pool sparkled with little fish that shone like stars. They circled {toy}'s reflection like a constellation.
The wind carried a song that the cave made by itself, with no instrument. {toy} hummed along.
Both paths met at a small wooden chest. Inside: a single perfect shell, smooth as a tooth.
{toy} held the shell to its ear. The sea whispered: come back any time. The end.
Tired and a little lost, {toy} stood before three doors in a strange village.
Behind the blue door: a kitchen full of the smell of bread. An old voice said: come in, come in.
Behind the green door: a garden of moonflowers nodding open. A cat waved with one slow paw.
Behind the yellow door: a room of soft cushions and a lamp turned low. Someone was already saving a seat.
Whichever door {toy} chose, it led to the same warm table — and the same kindly host who knew {toy}'s name. The end.
{toy} found a smooth flat pebble and stood by the pond, deciding what to do.
Plink, plink, plink — three skips! The pond clapped quietly with little ripples. The end.
The pebble sank slow and soft, and the rings spread out like a sleepy hello. The end.
{toy} yawned. The cat across the room yawned. The owl outside yawned too.
Where does the yawn go next?
The moon caught the yawn and tucked it into its smile. Goodnight. The end.
The river carried the yawn out to sea, where every wave nodded off. The end.
{toy} found a small {adjective} button on the floor of {room}. It seemed to wink.
What does {toy} wish?
A tiny crumb of cake appeared in {toy}'s palm. Just enough. {toy} grinned. The end.
A short hum, three notes long, danced through the room. {toy} hummed it back. The end.
A small cloud got lost from its sky and bumped softly against {toy}'s window.
{toy} cracked the window open. The cloud floated in, shy and damp.
How does {toy} help the cloud get home?
A breeze rolled through and the cloud rose up, waved a soft puff goodbye, and drifted home. The end.
{toy} sang gently. The cloud lifted, listened, and floated back to the sky humming along. The end.
{toy} lost one mitten on the way to the porch. Only one tiny {body_part} stayed warm.
Where does {toy} look?
The mitten was tucked beneath the {prop}, perfectly folded. A mouse had used it as a sleeping bag. The end.
The mitten waited behind the door, pretending to nap. {toy} put it on with a happy hum. The end.
Once a small puzzle box appeared on {toy}'s shelf. It would not open for hands.
It would not open for keys. It would not open for words.
But the box softened when {toy} pressed it gently with a {body_part}.
Inside lay a small note: 'You knew the answer all along — listen with your whole self.'
What does {toy} do with the note?

choices.label: Read it aloud one more time

steps.text: {toy} slept with the note close. In dreams, the box opened a hundred more times, each holding a softer secret. The end.
Saying the words made them feel true. {toy} folded the note neatly and went out into the world a little braver. The end.
On a quiet hill stood a small chapel with exactly {count} candles flickering in its windows.
Each candle was lit by a different friend. Each carried a wish.
{toy} climbed the hill with a candle of its own to add.
What is {toy}'s wish?
{toy} placed the candle with the others. Its flame leaned toward the sunrise, ready. The end.
The flame burned softer than the rest, but warmer somehow. Somewhere far away, a friend felt unexpectedly held. The end.
Once upon a time, {toy} could do almost anything except {action_verb}.
An old crane on a stone bridge said: 'There are two ways to learn. Patient and brave. Both work.'
Which way does {toy} try?
Day after day, {toy} practiced for ten minutes. By the end of the month, it became natural.
{toy} tripped, fell, got back up, tripped, fell, got back up. By sundown, {toy} was doing it without thinking.
Both ways led {toy} back to the bridge. The crane bowed deeply. 'Now,' it said, 'teach a friend.' The end.
{toy} owned two things it loved: a {prop} and another {prop}. They were jealous of each other.
Each one wanted to be {toy}'s favorite. {toy} did not know what to do.
An old turtle laughed gently. 'Two things can be loved at once,' she said. 'Watch.'
She placed both {prop}s side by side and told them a story about belonging.
How does the story end for the {prop}s?
The two {prop}s leaned together and never argued again. {toy} carried both proudly. The end.
They learned to share the shelf without bickering. {toy} loved both equally — and they finally believed it. The end.
One morning, {toy} woke up to find {room} entirely {adjective}. Even the dust was {adjective}.
Nothing was missing. Nothing was broken. It was just... very {adjective}.
How does {toy} respond?
{toy} discovered a sleepy fairy in the closet. She had spilled her bottle of feelings. By teatime, things were normal again.
{toy} spent the day in the {adjective} {room}, soaking it in. It faded by sundown, but the memory stayed bright. The end.
Far away in a village called Plinth, there was one day each year when everyone had to {action_verb} together.
{toy} arrived in Plinth on exactly that day, a stranger and a little nervous.
The mayor handed {toy} a small ribbon. 'You'll need this,' she said. 'It helps.'
How does {toy} join in?
A kind farmer offered an arm. Together they {action_verb}'d in perfect rhythm and laughed the whole time. The end.
{toy} {action_verb}'d in the middle of the square with quiet confidence. The whole village cheered. The end.
When {toy} woke up, there was a map drawn lightly on its {body_part}, in faint silver lines.
It showed a path through {room}, ending at a place {toy} had never noticed before.
Does {toy} follow the map?
The map led to a small loose floorboard. Beneath it: a tin of letters from someone who used to live here. The end.
By the time {toy} finished toast, the lines had faded. But {toy} remembered every turn — and found the spot. The end.
Past the edge of town, just past the old fence, a circus tent appeared only on nights without a moon.
{toy} found the entrance by accident, chasing a runaway balloon.
A small ticket-taker, half rabbit, half something else, asked: 'Do you have a story?'
{toy} told a small one — about a lost shoe and a found friend. The ticket-taker nodded, satisfied.
Inside, the air smelled like cinnamon and something a little older. A row of seats waited.
The first act was three foxes balancing teacups. The teacups were full. Nothing spilled.
The second act was a very tall bear who recited a poem about wind.
What is the final act?
Stars hung in the tent like ornaments. Two acrobats swung from one to the next, bowing as the lights twinkled. {toy} clapped softly. The end.
A kitten walked the entire length of a yarn tightrope, never wobbling. The crowd held its breath. {toy} did too. The end.
Captain Wren and her crew set out across the Quiet Sea looking for the singing islands.
{toy} was the smallest sailor on board, in charge of the lookout perch.
On the third day they passed a fog so thick the ship sailed by ear alone.
On the seventh day, a whale offered them directions in a slow, kind voice.
On the tenth day, {toy} spotted a green smudge on the horizon. 'Land!'
Which island do they visit first?
The island sang in deep, warm tones. The crew danced slowly along the beach.
Tiny bells hung from every tree. A breeze made the whole island chime. {toy} laughed in delight.
Even the stones hummed faintly. {toy} sat down and listened until the sun dipped low.
All three islands were waiting for them eventually. The crew sailed home with a song in every pocket. The end.
In the back room of an old shop, {toy} found a tiny clockwork bird, perfectly still.
Its wings were silver, its eyes were two tiny seeds. A note read: 'Wind once, then wait.'
{toy} turned the small key on its back. Tick. Tick. Tick.
The bird's eyes blinked. It tilted its head, looking at {toy} as if remembering.
It hopped to the windowsill. It looked at the sky. Then it looked back.
It wanted to go. But it also wanted to stay. The note had not said which.
What does {toy} do?
The bird flew once around {toy}'s head, dipped low in thanks, and zipped out into the bright air. {toy} smiled. The end.
The bird settled in, fluffed its silver wings, and sang one slow note before falling still. A keeper, then. The end.
Deep in a forest no map showed, every tree knew the name of someone who once stood beneath it.
{toy} entered with a small list of friends to find.
The first tree was a willow, who whispered the name of {toy}'s grandmother.
The second was an oak, who whispered the name of an old teacher.
The third was a small birch with a name {toy} hadn't expected to hear.
It was {toy}'s own name — though much younger, when first the trees had heard it.
How does {toy} respond?
{toy} rested against the trunk. The birch hummed softly all afternoon, as if it had missed {toy} too. The end.
{toy} whispered a friend's name into the birch's bark. Years from now, that friend would arrive and feel suddenly welcome. The end.

templates.title: The hot air balloon to nowhere

steps.text: {toy} climbed into a basket beneath an enormous striped balloon. The pilot was a grumpy badger named Felix.
'Where to?' Felix asked, lighting the burner. {toy} did not know.
'Then nowhere,' Felix grunted. 'Nowhere is my favorite place.'
Up they went. Up and up, until the houses became small and the rivers became ribbons.
Felix passed {toy} a thermos of cocoa. They drifted in silence for a while.
What does {toy} ask Felix?

choices.label: What he sees from up here

steps.text: Felix smiled — the first smile of the trip. 'Because nowhere never asks you to be anyone in particular.'
Felix pointed slowly: a heron, a fox kit, a kid waving from a yard. 'Everything,' he said. 'I see everything.'
Hours later they touched down in a meadow. {toy} hopped out feeling lighter than the basket itself. The end.
Mrs. Periwinkle's pet went missing in {room}. {toy} agreed to help search.
Behind the {prop}, {toy} found tiny prints leading toward a vent. The trail was warm.
On a high shelf, two yellow eyes blinked at {toy} from a pile of soft sweaters.
By the food bowl, the pet had simply been napping the whole time, ear twitching.
{toy} carried the pet back to Mrs. Periwinkle, who was overjoyed. She gave {toy} a slice of pie. The end.
On the night of the lantern festival, every house gave {toy} one paper lantern to release.
From the bridge, the lantern joined a hundred others drifting along the river, glowing soft and steady.
From the hill, the lantern rose into the dark sky, climbing slowly until it joined the stars.
Wherever it went, {toy}'s wish went with it. The night smelled like honey and woodsmoke. The end.
Three squirrels held a contest each year. This year, {toy} was invited to be the judge.
The squirrels lined up their piles. The biggest pile belonged to the smallest squirrel — Beanie. {toy} clapped.
One squirrel leapt from a low branch. Another from a high one. A third just leapt straight up. All three wobbled and grinned.
Each squirrel showed {toy} a hiding spot. {toy} promised to keep all three secret, even from the wind.
{toy} gave every squirrel a tiny ribbon. 'Three winners,' {toy} declared. The squirrels chittered with joy. The end.
An old well in the meadow only gave water to those who solved its daily riddle. Today's riddle: a {adjective} thing that grows when shared.
The well bubbled and said, 'Yes — and also.' It accepted the answer kindly.
The well laughed back, water splashing high. 'A clever one,' it murmured.
The well hummed in agreement. 'A musician's well today, then.'
{toy} drew up a bucket of cool, clear water and shared it with thirsty friends back home. The end.
After dark, the garden behind {room} woke up. The flowers walked. {toy} had to choose where to wander.
The roses poured tiny dewdrop teas. {toy} sipped one and felt very fancy.
The sunflower gave a long speech on the proper angle of leaves. {toy} took polite notes on a leaf.
Just before the moon set, all the flowers bowed and froze still. {toy} crept back to bed, smiling. The end.
A traveling wish-seller offered {toy} three balloons. Only one held a true wish.
Red was warm and bright. {toy} held it carefully and felt its small weight.
Yellow was light and quick. {toy} held its string and felt the breeze tug.
Blue was cool and quiet. {toy} held it and felt strangely seen.
When {toy} released the balloon, the sky brightened with one tiny new star. The wish-seller winked. They were all true, after all. The end.
A letter addressed to {toy} arrived a full day early. The postmark was tomorrow's.
Inside was a note: 'You will be brave today.' {toy} squared its shoulders, ready for whatever came.
{toy} placed the letter under the lamp and went about the day. The waiting felt patient and warm.
By afternoon, {toy} understood: the bravery was in choosing what to do with a note from the future. Either way was right. The end.
Somebody was stealing lullabies from the village. By bedtime, no one could remember a single tune.
{toy} hummed a brand new tune very softly. A small velvet creature crept out and tried to gather it up.
Past midnight, {toy} heard the softest rustling and saw a small velvet creature scooping up an old lullaby like a leaf.
The creature wasn't stealing — it had been collecting lost ones to bring back. It returned every tune by morning. The end.

templates.title: The train that only ran on Tuesdays

steps.text: A small train arrived at the platform every Tuesday afternoon and only on Tuesdays. {toy} climbed aboard.
Outside the window, the world slowly turned to a watercolor — soft and just a little blurred. {toy} pressed its nose to the glass.
In the dining car, a fox waiter served tea and a slice of clouds-and-cream pie. {toy} took small grateful bites.
The train stopped at a sunlit village neither on any map. {toy} stepped out and waved as it pulled away. The end.
A polite dragon named Saskia invited {toy} to tea at the top of a hill. {toy} brought a small gift.
Saskia spread the jam on a piece of dragon toast. 'You have excellent taste,' she rumbled, pleased.
Saskia read three poems aloud, her voice deep and slow. {toy} listened with both ears wide open.
Saskia tucked the mint into her teapot. 'A perfect afternoon needs a perfect garnish,' she said.
They sat together as the sun lowered, sipping tea and watching the valley turn gold. {toy} promised to come again. The end.
